chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Accessible Computing. (3-0). Credit 3. Exploration of the characteristics of traditionally disenfranchised user populations due to disability including discrimination; universal design concepts; exploration of ethical and legal motivations for creating accessible technology; development, evaluation, design, and implementation of equitable and inclusive software and computer based solutions; study of multiple existing accessibility standards. Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in CSCE 315 or CSCE 331.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade of C or better in CSCE 315 or CSCE 331.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
• Analyze common classes of disabilities with respect to culture, identity, and typical challenges in digital environments, and propose appropriate mitigation strategies.
• Justify the ethical and practical need for accessibility to employers and clients using evidence-based arguments and effective communication strategies.
• Interpret and evaluate key legal frameworks related to disability and accessibility, and assess their implications for technology development.
• Compare and critique different models of disability, including the civil rights model, and analyze their impact on approaches to accessibility.
• Apply and evaluate relevant accessibility standards in the design and implementation of technology solutions.
• Assess facilitators and barriers to technology use, and design or recommend strategies that reduce barriers and support accessibility for diverse users.
• Develop accessible technologies, documents, and presentations that comply with established standards, and evaluate their effectiveness for people with disabilities as well as other populations.
• Critique technology, identifying instances where accessibility standards may be insufficient, and propose design alternatives that fulfill the underlying goals of accessibility.
• Design, implement, and evaluate solutions that enable equitable interaction, mediating challenges and barriers for specific disabilities, and justify design choices within the framework of universal design principles.

chunks.title: Special Course Designation
chunks.content: None

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Graded work modifications
If you want to challenge any grading, you must do so within one week of when the grade is published.
We reserve the right to audit the grades for any assignments submitted to this course. During the audit process, we can decrease or increase your score. This could result in lowering the score of already released grades.

Grade Weighting

chunks.title: Pre-Class Engagement (CSCE 432: 10%, CSCE 632: 5%)
chunks.content:
Pre-class engagement can include but is not limited to course readings, quizzes, polls, and surveys.
• You get credit by submitting engagement submissions prior to the start of class.
• At the end of the semester, we will calculate your completion rate and add 15% capped at 100%.
• This allows you to miss some pre-class engagements without negatively impacting your score. For example, a friend has a personal crisis, and you choose to console them instead of doing some pre-class engagements. You do not have to ask the instructor for special consideration since it is built into the course.

chunks.title: Group Class Activities (10%)
chunks.content:
You will work with people at your table or move to a table so that there are at least 3 people per group.
• You will get credit if you attend class and you provide what is required for the activity.
• At the end of the semester, we will calculate your completion rate and add 15% capped at 100%.
• This allows you to miss some group class activities without negatively impacting your score. For example, you had a flat tire on the way to campus that caused you to miss a group class activity. You do not have to ask the instructor for special consideration since it is built into the course.

chunks.title: Assignments (25%)
chunks.content:
You will complete assignments throughout the semester either individually or with others as specified in the assignment. Note that assignments done with others are not a part of the group project.
• Some assignments will be peer graded.
1. Your score will be based on peer evaluations as well as your evaluation of other student assignments.
■ You can lose points for grading significantly differently than your peers or giving all students the same scores. (E.g. You will need to grade critically rather than give everyone full credit.)

chunks.title: Quick Applications (20%)
chunks.content: Timed assignments that have you do quick application of class materials. They are designed to take 15 to 20 minutes while you will have at least 45 minutes to complete them.

chunks.title: Literature Review (CSCE 432: 0%, CSCE 632: 10%)
chunks.content:
Students will compile a literature review on an accessibility topic comprised of at least 10 related peer reviewed conference or journal papers.
The literature review will clearly lay out
• themes and gaps in the research supported by evidence from the papers
• an annotated bibliography of each paper cited in the review

chunks.title: Current Research Presentation (CSCE 432: 10%, CSCE 632: 5%)
chunks.content:
You will present a summary of HCI research.
• Section
• 432: Your presentation will be based on a recent paper from a refereed source related to disability/accessibility.
• 632: Your presentation will be based on your Literature Review (See above.).
• Your presentation will be peer graded.
• Grading
• This will be peer graded.
■ Your score will be based on peer evaluations as well as your evaluation of other student assignments.
■ You can lose points for grading significantly differently than your peers or giving all students the same scores. (E.g. You will need to grade critically rather than give everyone full credit.)

chunks.title: Team Project (25%)
chunks.content:
You will work with a group to address a barrier encountered by people with disabilities that does not have an acceptable solution. Your team's barrier must be approved by the instructor. Teams must design and implement a technology based solution that provides equitable interactions for the barrier.
• Parts of this will be peer graded.
1. Your score will be based on peer evaluations as well as your evaluation of other student assignments.
■ You can lose points for grading significantly differently than your peers or giving all students the same scores. (E.g. You will need to grade critically rather than give everyone full credit.)
• The instructor will establish a multiplier for you based on your team peer evaluations.
1. The instructor can modify the multiplier at their discretion.
For example, if you are not actively participating with your group and you are assigned a multiplier of 0.9 and your group's final project score is 90, then your score would become 0.9*90 which is 81.

chunks.title: Grading Scale
chunks.content:
• Grades will be rounded to the nearest whole number.
• Grades will be assigned according to the following scale:
• A: ≥ 90
○ B: < 90 and ≥ 80
○ C: < 80 and ≥ 70
○ D: < 70 and ≥ 60
• F: < 60
• These grades represent varying degrees of achievement (see the university's grading system):
• A: excellent
• B: good
• C: satisfactory
• D: passing
• F: failing

chunks.title: Instructor Modifications
chunks.content:
• Instructor Modifications
• If warranted, the course instructor may assign a failing grade regardless of a student's computed final score.
• Failing to complete work can lead to letter grade reductions even if having a zero would still mathematically result in a higher letter grade.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
General G
General Guidelines
• It is your responsibility to keep up with the class, even when unexpected events interfere.
• All requests for extensions due to excused absences must follow the process for documenting absences below.
• Penalties for unexcused late submissions are 5% per calendar day, unless specified otherwise.
• Each student/team has a limited number of "late days" they may use by request as described below.

chunks.title: Late Days Table
chunks.content:
Summary of assignment types, late day allocations and limits.
| Assignment Type | Penalty for Late Work | Late Days Allotted | Max Late Days/Assignment | Late Day Request Process | Excused Absence Extension Process |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Assignments | 5% per calendar day | 6/student | 2 | Submit request via link on course Canvas homepage | Submit documentation via link on course Canvas homepage |
| Team Project | 5% per calendar day | 10/team | 2 | Submit request via link on course Canvas homepage | Discuss with instructor |
| Quick Applications | 5% per calendar day | 3/student | 2 | Submit request via link on course Canvas homepage | Submit documentation via link on course Canvas homepage |
| Pre-Class Engagement | Not accepted late | N/A | N/A | N/A | Submit documentation via link on course Canvas homepage |
| Group Class Activities | Not accepted late | N/A | N/A | N/A | Submit documentation via link on course Canvas homepage |
| Current Research Presentation | Not accepted late | N/A | N/A | N/A | Submit documentation via link on course Canvas homepage |
| Literature Review | Not accepted late | N/A | N/A | N/A | Submit documentation v link on course Canvas homepage |

chunks.title: Submitting Late Work
chunks.content:
1. Submit late work as usual.
• Do not wait for approval of an excused absence since you might miss the extended deadline while you wait.
2. Submit a late day request (after submission) via the appropriate link on the course Canvas homepage.
3. Follow the process for documenting absences.
• Documentation accepted as excused will trigger the removal of any penalty already assessed.
4. For team projects, late days are limited per team, not per individual.

chunks.title: Documenting Absences
chunks.content:
• Must be documented via the Absence Documentation link on the course Canvas homepage.
• Do not send email. Instead put what you would have put in the email into a simple document and submit that.
• Note that documentation can be a rationale that you type into a document and submit.
• For example, you can say "I was sick".
Further medical documentation will be needed if you are sick too frequently.
• Excused absences may receive an extension for graded work.
○ Note that the instructor has the discretion to approve an excused absence when it does not fall into the automatic excused absences defined by the university.
• Teams are generally expected to complete work on time, even if a team member has an excused absence.
• Discuss team extensions for extenuating circumstances with the instructor.

chunks.title: Unexcused Absences:
chunks.content:
• Standard late penalties apply.
• Late days may be used up to the maximum outlined.

chunks.title: Extenuating Circumstances
chunks.content: For any questions or special circumstances, contact the instructor through Canvas, the class chat, office hours, or make an appointment.

chunks.title: Technology Requirements
chunks.content:
• Personal computer that fulfills the Texas A&M Computer Requirements.
• Used to consume content and interact with online course resources.
• Able to connect your computer to project via an HDMI cable.
• You will need an HDMI adapter if your device does not have an HDMI port.
• Wireless device to submit online forms during class.
• Cell phones and laptop computers are common options.
• Technology to Capture an Image
• At times you may be asked to create a sketch or diagram to submit electronically. You need the ability to get the diagram into a format that can be uploaded. You could sketch on paper and take a picture with your camera, sketch on a tablet device, or use a scanner. You should be able to do this quickly, especially if needed for an online timed submission.

chunks.title: Respect for All
chunks.content:
Howdy! Your CSCE 489 instructors want you to succeed in this challenging course. While the Disability Accommodation Portal (AIM Portal) makes it easier to share accommodation information, we ask that you also schedule a meeting with your section's instructor to discuss your accommodations in person. In addition to accommodations, we're happy to talk about strategies for succeeding in this course.
We strongly encourage you to secure your accommodations—even if you're unsure you'll need them. When Dr. Moore began teaching at Texas A&M, he noticed a concerning pattern: students often chose to take the first exam without using their approved accommodations. Many said they wanted to “try it without them." After the first exam, there was often a rush of accommodation requests. These instances have decreased significantly since we've been more explicit about our preferences.
While Dr. Moore suspects that some students hesitate due to negative experiences with previous instructors, we want you to know that our department culture is one of support. We are committed to providing accommodations approved by Disability Resources with professionalism and care.
"We prefer that you get your accommodations and not need them, rather than trying without your accommodations and finding out that you needed them.”
Finally, everyone should be mindful of creating software and digital artifacts that are accessible. If you're majoring in computer science or computer engineering, consider taking CSCE 432: Accessible Computing, a course developed by Dr. Moore to help students design with accessibility in mind.

chunks.title: Preferred Name and Preferred Gender Pronouns
chunks.content:
We strive to foster a respectful and inclusive learning environment. As part of professional courtesy and sensitivity, we encourage the use of preferred names and gender pronouns when interacting with others, especially in discussions involving race, culture, religion, politics, sexual orientation, gender identity, and nationality.
While participation in this practice is voluntary, we ask all students to approach these interactions with mutual respect and empathy, regardless of personal beliefs.
If you have a preferred name or pronouns you'd like us to use, please feel free to share them. We will make every reasonable effort to honor your preferences within the bounds of current law and university policy.

chunks.title: Non-Discrimination Policy
chunks.content:
Texas A&M is committed to the fundamental principles of academic freedom, equality of opportunity and human dignity. To fulfill its multiple missions as an institution of higher learning, Texas A&M encourages a climate that values and nurtures collegiality, diversity, pluralism and the uniqueness of the individual within our state, nation and world. All decisions and actions involving students and employees should be based on applicable law and individual merit.
Texas A&M University prohibits students, employees, and third parties from engaging in discrimination and/or harassment on the basis of race, color, sex, gender identity, age, religion, disability, national origin, sexual orientation, genetic information, veteran status, or any other characteristic protected by federal, state, or local law. In addition, acting in complicity with another who engages in any of these forms of prohibited conduct, or retaliating against a person who participates in protected activity, is also prohibited.
Harassment of a student in class, i.e., a pattern of behavior directed against a particular student with the intent of humiliating or intimidating that student will not be tolerated. The mere expression of one's ideas is not harassment and is fully protected by academic freedom, but personal harassment of individual students is not permitted.

chunks.title: Professionalism and Respect
chunks.content:
You are expected to treat your instructor and all other participants in the course with courtesy and respect. Your comments to others should be factual, constructive, and free from harassing statements. You are encouraged to disagree with other students, but such disagreements need to be based upon facts and documentation (rather than prejudices and personalities).
Professional and respectful engagement is expected at all times; failure to do so may impact assignment grades.
Students need to contribute in intelligent, positive, and constructive manners within the course. Behaviors that are abusive, disruptive, or harassing may result in disciplinary actions as specified within the Student Rules.
Sensitive or controversial topics might be discussed in this course. Comments posted within this course are to remain confidential to the audience of this course; do not copy or share messages or writings from this course with others not in this course section.
Students in this course are also responsible for being familiar with the University. student rules and policies.

chunks.title: Student Behavior & Academic Integrity
chunks.content:
Acknowledgement
By submitting anything to this course, electronically or otherwise, you are asserting the following: "On my honor, as an Aggie, I have neither given nor received unauthorized aid on this academic work. In particular, I certify that I have listed above all the sources that I consulted regarding this assignment and that I have not received or given any assistance that is contrary to the letter or the spirit of the collaboration guidelines for this assignment.”

chunks.title: Spirit & Intent
chunks.content: Assignments are designed to help you engage with HCl concepts. Doing anything to subvert the learning objectives harm your learning experience and constitute academic dishonesty.

chunks.title: Recording statement
chunks.content: Students may not record audio or video of any course activity unless the student has an approved accommodation from Disability Services permitting the recording of lectures and/or laboratory sessions. This accommodation letter must be presented to the instructor in advance of any recording being done. Students with permission to record classes are not permitted to redistribute audio or video recordings of statements or comments from the course to other individuals without the express permission of the faculty member and of any students who are recorded.

chunks.title: Plagiarism and Copyright
chunks.content: All course materials are copyrighted and may not be copied or distributed without permission. Plagiarism will be treated as a serious academic violation per university policies.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
With the rise of artificial intelligence (Al) technologies, our creative processes are evolving. However, these tools do not replace the essential skills of drafting, revising, and reflecting on texts, programs, and lectures. Nor do they substitute for the critical thinking required to locate, evaluate, incorporate, and cite sources responsibly.
Education is about preparing future innovators and leaders. That includes learning to use emerging technologies ethically and effectively. In this course, you are permitted to use generative Al tools (e.g., ChatGPT, Google Bard, DALL·E 2) to assist with assignments if you choose to. These tools can help you brainstorm ideas or provide feedback, but they should not complete your work for you.
To maintain academic integrity:
• Cite any Al-generated content that informs your work. This includes in-text citations, quotations, and entries in your reference list.
• Failure to properly attribute Al-generated material is considered academic dishonesty.
• No more than 30% of any assignment should consist of Al-generated text.
For guidance on citing generative Al using APA 7th edition, refer to the APA Style Blog for examples, including how to cite ChatGPT in both the body of your work and your reference list.
Please be aware of the limitations of generative Al:
• It often recycles existing ideas rather than producing original thought.
• It may present false or misleading information alongside accurate content.
• It can hallucinate—creating citations or facts that do not exist.
• It may restructure your writing in ways that distort your intended meaning.
Always review and critically evaluate any Al-generated content before including it in your work.

chunks.title: University Policies
chunks.content: This section outlines the university-level policies that must be included in each course syllabus. The TAMU Faculty Advisory Council established the wording of these policies.

chunks.title: Academic Integrity Statement and Policy
chunks.content:
"An Aggie does not lie, cheat or steal, or tolerate those who do.”
“Texas A&M University students are responsible for authenticating all work submitted to an instructor. If asked, students must be able to produce proof that the item submitted is indeed the work of that student. Students must keep appropriate records at all times. The inability to authenticate one's work, should the instructor request it, may be sufficient grounds to initiate an academic misconduct case” (Section 20.1.2.3, Student Rule 20).
You can learn more about the Aggie Honor System Office Rules and Procedures, academic integrity, and your rights and responsibilities at aggiehonor.tamu.edu.

chunks.title: University Attendance Policy
chunks.content:
The university views class attendance and participation as an individual student responsibility. Students are expected to attend class and to complete all assignments.
Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for information about excused absences, including definitions, and related documentation and timelines.

chunks.title: Course Specific Attendance Policy
chunks.content: You are required to attend every class, arrive on time, and stay the whole time. You are responsible for learning the material covered in class, regardless of your attendance.

chunks.title: Makeup Work Policy
chunks.content:
Students will be excused from attending class on the day of a graded activity or when attendance contributes to a student's grade, for the reasons stated in Student Rule 7, or other reason deemed appropriate by the instructor.
Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for information about makeup work, including definitions, and related documentation and timelines.
Absences related to Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972 may necessitate a period of more than 30 days for make-up work, and the timeframe for make-up work should be agreed upon by the student and instructor” (Student Rule 7, Section 7.4.1).
“The instructor is under no obligation to provide an opportunity for the student to make up work missed because of an unexcused absence" (Student Rule 7, Section 7.4.2).
Students who request an excused absence are expected to uphold the Aggie Honor Code and Student Conduct Code. (See Student Rule 24.)

chunks.title: Course Specific Makeup Work Policy
chunks.content: See Course Specific Late Work Policy above.

chunks.title: Notice of Nondiscrimination
chunks.content:
Texas A&M University is committed to providing safe and non-discriminatory learning, living, and work environments for all members of the University community. The University provides equal opportunity to all employees, students, applicants for employment or admission, and the public, regardless of race, color, sex (including pregnancy and related conditions), religion, national origin, age, disability, genetic information, or veteran status.
Texas A&M University will promptly, thoroughly, and fairly investigate and resolve all complaints of discrimination, harassment (including sexual harassment), complicity, and related retaliation based on a protected class in accordance with System Regulation 08.01.01, University Rule 08.01.01.M1, Standard Administrative Procedure (SAP) 08.01.01.M1.01, and applicable federal and state laws. In accordance with Title IX and its implementing regulations, Texas A&M does not discriminate on the basis of sex in any educational program or activity, including admissions and employment.

chunks.title: Civil Rights, Free Speech, and Title IX Policies
chunks.content:
The following person has been designated to handle inquiries and complaints regarding the non-discrimination policies: Jennifer M. Smith, TAMU Associate VP & Title IX Coordinator at YMCA Ste 108, College Station, TX 77843, 979-458-8407, or email civilrights@tamu.edu. For other reporting options, visit the U.S. Department of Education Office for Civil Rights Complaint Assessment System to locate the address and phone number of the office that serves your area, or call 1-800-421-3481.
Texas A&M University is committed to fostering a learning environment that is safe and productive for all. University policies and federal and state laws prohibit discrimination and harassment based on an individual's race, color, sex, (including pregnancy and related conditions), religion, national origin, age, disability, genetic information, veteran status, or any other legally protected characteristic. This includes forms of sex-based violence, such as sexual assault, sexual harassment, sexual exploitation, dating/domestic violence, and stalking.
Students can report discrimination/harassment, access supportive resources, or learn more about their options for resolving complaints on the University's Civil Rights & Title IX webpage.
Students should be aware that all university employees (except medical or mental health providers) are mandatory reporters, which means that if they observe, experience or become aware of an incident that they reasonably believe to be discrimination/harassment alleged to have been committed by or against a person who was a student or employee at the time of the incident, the employee must report the incident to the university.

chunks.title: Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) Policy
chunks.content:
Texas A&M University is committed to providing equitable access to learning opportunities for all students. If you experience barriers to your education due to a disability or think you may have a disability, please contact the Disability Resources office on your campus (resources listed below). Disabilities may include, but are not limited to, attentional, learning, mental health, sensory, physical, or chronic health conditions. All students are encouraged to discuss their disability-related needs with Disability Resources and their instructors as soon as possible.
To request academic accommodations, contact the designated ADA office based on your location:
• Texas A&M University, College of Nursing, College of Dentistry, Irma Lerma Rangel College of Pharmacy College Station, College of Medicine, School of Public Health, Institute of Biosciences and Technology, EnMed Program, Bush School in Washington DC, Mays Business School – CityCentre, TAMU Engineering Academies, Texas A&M University Higher Education Center at McAllen and Texas A&M University at Galveston should contact Disability Resources at (979) 845-1637 or disability@tamu.edu.
• Texas A&M University School of Law should contact the Office of Student Affairs at (817) 212-4111 or law-disability@law.tamu.edu to request accommodations.
• Irma Lerma Rangel College of Pharmacy in Kingsville should contact the Disability Resource Center at Texas A&M University-Kingsville at (361) 593-3024 or drc.center@tamuk.edu to request accommodations.
• Texas A&M University College of Veterinary Medicine & Biomedical Sciences in Canyon should contact the Office of Student Accessibility at West Texas A&M University – Canyon at (806) 651-2335 or osa@wtamu.edu.
If you are experiencing difficulties with your approved accommodations, contact the office responsible for approving your accommodations or the Texas A&M ADA Coordinator Julie Kuder at ADA.Coordinator@tamu.edu or (979) 458-8407.

chunks.title: Pregnancy Accommodations
chunks.content:
Texas A&M provides reasonable accommodations to students due to pregnancy and/or related conditions, such as childbirth, recovery, and lactation. Students should contact the University's Pregnancy Coordinator as soon as they become aware of the need for accommodation. Depending on the circumstances, accommodations could include extended time to complete assignments or exams, changes in course sequence, or modifications to the physical classroom environment.
Texas A&M will also allow a voluntary leave of absence, ensure the availability of lactation space, and maintain grievance procedures to provide for the prompt and equitable resolution of complaints of sex discrimination. For information regarding pregnancy accommodations, email TIX.Pregnancy@tamu.edu.

chunks.title: Statement on Mental Health and Wellness
chunks.content:
Texas A&M University recognizes that mental health and wellness are critical factors influencing a student's academic success and overall wellbeing. Students are encouraged to engage in healthy self-care practices by utilizing the resources and services available through University Health Services. The TELUS Health Student Support app provides access to professional counseling in multiple languages anytime, anywhere by phone or chat, and the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline offers 24-hour emergency support at 988 or 988lifeline.org.
Texas A&M College Station
Students needing a listening ear can contact University Health Services at 979.458.4584. Call 911 or visit your nearest emergency room if you are currently experiencing a life-threatening situation or if your safety is at risk. 24-hour emergency help is also available through the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline (988) or at 988lifeline.org.

chunks.title: Statement on the Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA)
chunks.content:
FERPA is a federal law designed to protect the privacy of educational records by limiting access to these records, to establish the right of students to inspect and review their educational records, and to provide guidelines for the correction of inaccurate and misleading data through informal and formal hearings.
Currently enrolled students wishing to withhold any or all directory information items can do so within howdy.tamu.edu using the Directory Information Withholding Form. The complete FERPA Notice to Students and the student records policy is available on the Office of the Registrar webpage.
Items that can never be identified as public information are a student's social security number, citizenship, gender, grades, GPR, or class schedule. All efforts will be made in this class to protect your privacy and to ensure confidential treatment of information associated with or generated by your participation in the class.
Directory items include name, UIN, local address, permanent address, email address, local telephone number, permanent telephone number, dates of attendance, program of study (college, major, campus), classification, previous institutions attended, degrees, honors and awards received, participation in officially recognized activities and sports, medical residence location, and medical residence specialization.

chunks.title: Free Speech and Civil Discourse
chunks.content: Texas A&M recognizes that the pursuit of truth through open and robust discourse is critical to academic inquiry. However, as a community of scholars, the university has an aspirational expectation that such discourse will be conducted in accordance with Aggie Core Values. In this “marketplace of ideas,” we encourage civil dialogue creating an environment that allows individuals to express their ideas and to have their ideas challenged in respectful and responsible ways. Students can learn more about Freedom of Expression and Free Speech on the University's website about the First Amendment.
